revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

===Daishoji=== # [[Maeda Toshiharu]] (1618 – 1660) # [[Maeda Toshiaki I|Maeda Toshiaki]] I (1638 – 1692) # [[Maeda Toshinao]] (1672 – 1711) # [[Maeda Toshiakira]] (1691 – 1737) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] I (1733 – 1781) # [[Maeda Toshiaki II|Maeda Toshiaki]] II (1758 – 1791) # [[Maeda Toshitane]] (1760 – 1788) # [[Maeda Toshiyasu (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1779 – 1806) # [[Maeda Toshikore]] (1785 – 1837) # [[Maeda Toshinaka]] (1812 – 1838) # [[Maeda Toshihira]] (1824 – 1849) # [[Maeda Toshinori (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1833 – 1855) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] II (1835 – 1855) # [[Maeda Toshika]] (1841 – 1920) # [[Maeda Toshimitsu]] (1905-?) # [[Maeda Toshihiro]] (b.1929)

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

===Toyama=== # [[Maeda Toshitsugu]] (1617 – 1674) # [[:en:Maeda Masatoshi|Maeda Masatoshi]] (1649 – 1706) # [[Maeda Toshioki]] (1678 – 1733) # [[Maeda Toshitaka (Toyama)|Maeda Toshitaka]] (1690 – 1745) # [[Maeda Toshiyuki]] (1730 – 1762) # [[Maeda Toshitomo]] (1737 – 1794) # [[Maeda Toshihisa]] (1762 – 1787) # [[Maeda Toshinori (Toyama)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1768 – 1801) # [[Maeda Toshitsuyo]] (1772 – 1836) # [[Maeda Toshiyasu (Toyama)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1800-1859) # [[Maeda Toshitomo]] (1834 – 1854) # [[Maeda Toshikata]] (1835 – 1904) # [[Maeda Toshiatsu]] (1856 – 1921) # [[Maeda Toshio (Toyama)|Maeda Toshio]] (1886 – 1966) # [[Maeda Toshinobu]] # [[Maeda Akitoshi]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

===Toyama=== # [[Maeda Toshitsugu]] (1617 – 1674) # [[:en:Maeda Masatoshi|Maeda Masatoshi]] (1649 – 1706) # [[Maeda Toshioki]] (1678 – 1733) # [[Maeda Toshitaka (Toyama)|Maeda Toshitaka]] (1690 – 1745) # [[Maeda Toshiyuki]] (1730 – 1762) # [[Maeda Toshitomo]] (1737 – 1794) # [[Maeda Toshihisa]] (1762 – 1787) # [[Maeda Toshinori (Toyama)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1768 – 1801) # [[Maeda Toshitsuyo]] (1772 – 1836) # [[Maeda Toshiyasu (Toyama)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1800-1859) # [[Maeda Toshitomo]] (1834 – 1854) # [[Maeda Toshikata]] (1835 – 1904) # [[Maeda Toshiatsu]] (1856 – 1921) # [[Maeda Toshio (Toyama)|Maeda Toshio]] (1886 – 1966) # [[Maeda Toshinobu]] # [[Maeda Akitoshi]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

===Toyama=== # [[Maeda Toshitsugu]] (1617 – 1674) # [[:en:Maeda Masatoshi|Maeda Masatoshi]] (1649 – 1706) # [[Maeda Toshioki]] (1678 – 1733) # [[Maeda Toshitaka (Toyama)|Maeda Toshitaka]] (1690 – 1745) # [[Maeda Toshiyuki]] (1730 – 1762) # [[Maeda Toshitomo]] (1737 – 1794) # [[Maeda Toshihisa]] (1762 – 1787) # [[Maeda Toshinori (Toyama)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1768 – 1801) # [[Maeda Toshitsuyo]] (1772 – 1836) # [[Maeda Toshiyasu (Toyama)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1800-1859) # [[Maeda Toshitomo]] (1834 – 1854) # [[Maeda Toshikata]] (1835 – 1904) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiatsu|Maeda Toshiatsu]] (1856 – 1921) # [[Maeda Toshio (Toyama)|Maeda Toshio]] (1886 – 1966) # [[Maeda Toshinobu]] # [[Maeda Akitoshi]]
===Daishoji=== # [[:en:Maeda Toshiharu|Maeda Toshiharu]] (1618 – 1660) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiaki I|Maeda Toshiaki]] I (1638 – 1692) # [[Maeda Toshinao]] (1672 – 1711) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiakira|Maeda Toshiakira]] (1691 – 1737) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] I (1733 – 1781) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiaki II|Maeda Toshiaki]] II (1758 – 1791) # [[Maeda Toshitane]] (1760 – 1788) # [[Maeda Toshiyasu (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1779 – 1806) # [[Maeda Toshikore]] (1785 – 1837) # [[Maeda Toshinaka]] (1812 – 1838) # [[Maeda Toshihira]] (1824 – 1849) # [[Maeda Toshinori (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1833 – 1855) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] II (1835 – 1855) # [[Maeda Toshika]] (1841 – 1920) # [[Maeda Toshimitsu]] (1905-?) # [[Maeda Toshihiro]] (b.1929)

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

===Toyama=== # [[Maeda Toshitsugu]] (1617 – 1674) # [[:en:Maeda Masatoshi|Maeda Masatoshi]] (1649 – 1706) # [[Maeda Toshioki]] (1678 – 1733) # [[Maeda Toshitaka (Toyama)|Maeda Toshitaka]] (1690 – 1745) # [[Maeda Toshiyuki]] (1730 – 1762) # [[Maeda Toshitomo]] (1737 – 1794) # [[:en:Maeda Toshihisa|Maeda Toshihisa]] (1762 – 1787) # [[Maeda Toshinori (Toyama)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1768 – 1801) # [[Maeda Toshitsuyo]] (1772 – 1836) # [[Maeda Toshiyasu (Toyama)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1800-1859) # [[Maeda Toshitomo]] (1834 – 1854) # [[Maeda Toshikata]] (1835 – 1904) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiatsu|Maeda Toshiatsu]] (1856 – 1921) # [[Maeda Toshio (Toyama)|Maeda Toshio]] (1886 – 1966) # [[Maeda Toshinobu]] # [[Maeda Akitoshi]]
===Daishoji=== # [[:en:Maeda Toshiharu|Maeda Toshiharu]] (1618 – 1660) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiaki I|Maeda Toshiaki]] I (1638 – 1692) # [[Maeda Toshinao]] (1672 – 1711) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiakira|Maeda Toshiakira]] (1691 – 1737) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] I (1733 – 1781) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiaki II|Maeda Toshiaki]] II (1758 – 1791) # [[Maeda Toshitane]] (1760 – 1788) # [[Maeda Toshiyasu (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1779 – 1806) # [[Maeda Toshikore]] (1785 – 1837) # [[Maeda Toshinaka]] (1812 – 1838) # [[:en:Maeda Toshihira|Maeda Toshihira]] (1824 – 1849) # [[Maeda Toshinori (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1833 – 1855) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] II (1835 – 1855) # [[Maeda Toshika]] (1841 – 1920) # [[Maeda Toshimitsu]] (1905-?) # [[Maeda Toshihiro]] (b.1929)

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[:en:Maeda Toshiie|Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

===Toyama=== # [[Maeda Toshitsugu]] (1617 – 1674) # [[:en:Maeda Masatoshi|Maeda Masatoshi]] (1649 – 1706) # [[Maeda Toshioki]] (1678 – 1733) # [[Maeda Toshitaka (Toyama)|Maeda Toshitaka]] (1690 – 1745) # [[Maeda Toshiyuki]] (1730 – 1762) # [[Maeda Toshitomo]] (1737 – 1794) # [[:en:Maeda Toshihisa|Maeda Toshihisa]] (1762 – 1787) # [[Maeda Toshinori (Toyama)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1768 – 1801) # [[Maeda Toshitsuyo]] (1772 – 1836) # [[Maeda Toshiyasu (Toyama)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1800-1859) # [[Maeda Toshitomo]] (1834 – 1854) # [[Maeda Toshikata]] (1835 – 1904) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiatsu|Maeda Toshiatsu]] (1856 – 1921) # [[Maeda Toshio (Toyama)|Maeda Toshio]] (1886 – 1966) # [[Maeda Toshinobu]] # [[Maeda Akitoshi]]
===Daishoji=== # [[:en:Maeda Toshiharu|Maeda Toshiharu]] (1618 – 1660) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiaki I|Maeda Toshiaki]] I (1638 – 1692) # [[Maeda Toshinao]] (1672 – 1711) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiakira|Maeda Toshiakira]] (1691 – 1737) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] I (1733 – 1781) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiaki II|Maeda Toshiaki]] II (1758 – 1791) # [[Maeda Toshitane]] (1760 – 1788) # [[Maeda Toshiyasu (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1779 – 1806) # [[Maeda Toshikore]] (1785 – 1837) # [[Maeda Toshinaka]] (1812 – 1838) # [[:en:Maeda Toshihira|Maeda Toshihira]] (1824 – 1849) # [[Maeda Toshinori (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1833 – 1855) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] II (1835 – 1855) # [[:en:Maeda Toshika|Maeda Toshika]] (1841 – 1920) # [[Maeda Toshimitsu]] (1905-?) # [[Maeda Toshihiro]] (b.1929)

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[:en:Maeda Toshiie|Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[Meiji period]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

===Toyama=== # [[Maeda Toshitsugu]] (1617 – 1674) # [[:en:Maeda Masatoshi|Maeda Masatoshi]] (1649 – 1706) # [[Maeda Toshioki]] (1678 – 1733) # [[Maeda Toshitaka (Toyama)|Maeda Toshitaka]] (1690 – 1745) # [[Maeda Toshiyuki]] (1730 – 1762) # [[Maeda Toshitomo]] (1737 – 1794) # [[:en:Maeda Toshihisa|Maeda Toshihisa]] (1762 – 1787) # [[Maeda Toshinori (Toyama)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1768 – 1801) # [[Maeda Toshitsuyo]] (1772 – 1836) # [[Maeda Toshiyasu (Toyama)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1800-1859) # [[Maeda Toshitomo]] (1834 – 1854) # [[:en:Maeda Toshikata|Maeda Toshikata]] (1835 – 1904) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiatsu|Maeda Toshiatsu]] (1856 – 1921) # [[Maeda Toshio (Toyama)|Maeda Toshio]] (1886 – 1966) # [[Maeda Toshinobu]] # [[Maeda Akitoshi]]
===Daishoji=== # [[:en:Maeda Toshiharu|Maeda Toshiharu]] (1618 – 1660) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiaki I|Maeda Toshiaki]] I (1638 – 1692) # [[Maeda Toshinao]] (1672 – 1711) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiakira|Maeda Toshiakira]] (1691 – 1737) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] I (1733 – 1781) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiaki II|Maeda Toshiaki]] II (1758 – 1791) # [[Maeda Toshitane]] (1760 – 1788) # [[Maeda Toshiyasu (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1779 – 1806) # [[:en:Maeda Toshikore|Maeda Toshikore]] (1785 – 1837) # [[Maeda Toshinaka]] (1812 – 1838) # [[:en:Maeda Toshihira|Maeda Toshihira]] (1824 – 1849) # [[Maeda Toshinori (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1833 – 1855) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] II (1835 – 1855) # [[:en:Maeda Toshika|Maeda Toshika]] (1841 – 1920) # [[Maeda Toshimitsu]] (1905-?) # [[Maeda Toshihiro]] (b.1929)
