<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q578033]] Wikidata name: Maeda clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[:en:Sugawara no Kiyotomo|Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[:en:Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[:en:Maeda Toshiie|Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[:en:Maeda Toshimasu|Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[:en:Takigawa Kazumasu|Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[:en:Mogami clan|Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[:en:Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[:en:Maeda Toshitsune|Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[:en:Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[:en:Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[:en:Nanokaichi Domain|Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.
==Head Family== ===Owari-Arako=== # [[:en:Maeda Toshitaka (Toyama)|Maeda Toshitaka]] # [[Maeda Toshimasa]] # [[:en:Maeda Toshihisa|Maeda Toshihisa]] (d.1587) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiie|Maeda Toshiie]] # [[Maeda Hidetsugu]] (d. 1586)

===Kaga=== [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|240px|thumb|Kaga-Maeda family crest]] # [[:en:Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]] # [[Maeda Toshimasa]] # [[:en:Maeda Toshiie|Maeda Toshiie]] # [[Maeda Toshinaga]] # [[:en:Maeda Toshitsune|Maeda Toshitsune]] # [[:en:Maeda Mitsutaka|Maeda Mitsutaka]] # [[:en:Maeda Tsunanori|Maeda Tsunanori]] # [[:en:Maeda Yoshinori|Maeda Yoshinori]] # [[:en:Maeda Munetoki|Maeda Munetoki]] # [[:en:Maeda Shigehiro|Maeda Shigehiro]] # [[:en:Maeda Shigenobu|Maeda Shigenobu]] # [[:en:Maeda Shigemichi|Maeda Shigemichi]] # [[:en:Maeda Harunaga|Maeda Harunaga]] # [[:en:Maeda Narinaga|Maeda Narinaga]] # [[:en:Maeda Nariyasu|Maeda Nariyasu]] # [[Maeda Yoshiyasu]] # [[:en:Maeda Toshitsugu|Maeda Toshitsugu]] (1858-1900) # [[:en:Toshinari Maeda|Toshinari Maeda]] (1885-1942) # [[Toshitatsu Maeda]] (1908-1989) # [[Toshiyasu Maeda]] (b.1935) # [[Toshinori Maeda]] (b.1963, Heir) # [[Toshiyuki Maeda]] (2nd generation heir) # [[Toshiyoshi Maeda (President-elect, President and CEO of Inoda Coffee)]]
===Toyama=== # [[:en:Maeda Toshitsugu|Maeda Toshitsugu]] (1617 – 1674) # [[:en:Maeda Masatoshi|Maeda Masatoshi]] (1649 – 1706) # [[:en:Maeda Toshioki|Maeda Toshioki]] (1678 – 1733) # [[:en:Maeda Toshitaka (Toyama)|Maeda Toshitaka]] (1690 – 1745) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiyuki|Maeda Toshiyuki]] (1730 – 1762) # [[:en:Maeda Toshitomo|Maeda Toshitomo]] (1737 – 1794) # [[:en:Maeda Toshihisa|Maeda Toshihisa]] (1762 – 1787) # [[:en:Maeda Toshinori (Toyama)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1768 – 1801) # [[:en:Maeda Toshitsuyo|Maeda Toshitsuyo]] (1772 – 1836) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiyasu (Toyama)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1800-1859) # [[:en:Maeda Toshitomo|Maeda Toshitomo]] (1834 – 1854) # [[:en:Maeda Toshikata|Maeda Toshikata]] (1835 – 1904) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiatsu|Maeda Toshiatsu]] (1856 – 1921) # [[Maeda Toshio (Toyama)|Maeda Toshio]] (1886 – 1966) # [[Maeda Toshinobu]] # [[Maeda Akitoshi]]
===Daishoji=== # [[:en:Maeda Toshiharu|Maeda Toshiharu]] (1618 – 1660) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiaki I|Maeda Toshiaki]] I (1638 – 1692) # [[:en:Maeda Toshinao|Maeda Toshinao]] (1672 – 1711) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiakira|Maeda Toshiakira]] (1691 – 1737) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] I (1733 – 1781) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiaki II|Maeda Toshiaki]] II (1758 – 1791) # [[:en:Maeda Toshitane|Maeda Toshitane]] (1760 – 1788) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiyasu (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1779 – 1806) # [[:en:Maeda Toshikore|Maeda Toshikore]] (1785 – 1837) # [[:en:Maeda Toshinaka|Maeda Toshinaka]] (1812 – 1838) # [[:en:Maeda Toshihira|Maeda Toshihira]] (1824 – 1849) # [[:en:Maeda Toshinori (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1833 – 1855) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] II (1835 – 1855) # [[:en:Maeda Toshika|Maeda Toshika]] (1841 – 1920) # [[Maeda Toshimitsu]] (1905-?) # [[Maeda Toshihiro]] (b.1929)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q578033]] Wikidata name: Maeda clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[:en:Sugawara no Kiyotomo|Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[:en:Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[:en:Maeda Toshiie|Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[:en:Maeda Toshimasu|Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[:en:Takigawa Kazumasu|Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[:en:Mogami clan|Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[:en:Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[:en:Maeda Toshitsune|Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[:en:Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[:en:Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[:en:Nanokaichi Domain|Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[:en:Sugawara no Kiyotomo|Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[:en:Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[:en:Maeda Toshiie|Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[:en:Maeda Toshimasu|Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[:en:Takigawa Kazumasu|Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[:en:Mogami clan|Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[:en:Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[:en:Maeda Toshitsune|Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[:en:Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[:en:Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[:en:Nanokaichi Domain|Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q578033]] Wikidata name: Maeda clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[:en:Sugawara no Kiyotomo|Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[:en:Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[:en:Maeda Toshiie|Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[:en:Maeda Toshimasu|Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[:en:Takigawa Kazumasu|Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[:en:Mogami clan|Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[:en:Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[:en:Maeda Toshitsune|Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[:en:Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[:en:Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[:en:Nanokaichi Domain|Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q578033]] Wikidata name: Maeda clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]] |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ([[:en:Abolition of the han system|Abolition of the han system]]) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] clan who occupied most of the [[:en:Hokuriku region|Hokuriku region]] of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[:en:Sugawara no Kiyotomo|Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the [[:en:Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi–Momoyama period]]. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[:en:Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[:en:Maeda Toshiie|Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[:en:Maeda Toshimasu|Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[:en:Takigawa Kazumasu|Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the [[:en:Mogami clan|Mogami clan]] leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under [[:en:Oda Nobunaga|Oda Nobunaga]]. He began his career as a [[:en:Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and built [[:en:Kanazawa Castle|Kanazawa Castle]]; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in [[:en:Kanazawa|Kanazawa]] from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. [[:en:Maeda Toshitsune|Maeda Toshitsune]] established two cadet branches of the clan at [[:en:Toyama Domain|Toyama]] and [[:en:Daishōji Domain|Daishōji]]. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[:en:Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[:en:Nanokaichi Domain|Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

===Kaga=== [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|240px|thumb|Kaga-Maeda family crest]] # [[:en:Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]] # [[Maeda Toshimasa]] # [[:en:Maeda Toshiie|Maeda Toshiie]] # [[Maeda Toshinaga]] # [[:en:Maeda Toshitsune|Maeda Toshitsune]] # [[:en:Maeda Mitsutaka|Maeda Mitsutaka]] # [[:en:Maeda Tsunanori|Maeda Tsunanori]] # [[:en:Maeda Yoshinori|Maeda Yoshinori]] # [[:en:Maeda Munetoki|Maeda Munetoki]] # [[:en:Maeda Shigehiro|Maeda Shigehiro]] # [[:en:Maeda Shigenobu|Maeda Shigenobu]] # [[:en:Maeda Shigemichi|Maeda Shigemichi]] # [[:en:Maeda Harunaga|Maeda Harunaga]] # [[:en:Maeda Narinaga|Maeda Narinaga]] # [[:en:Maeda Nariyasu|Maeda Nariyasu]] # [[Maeda Yoshiyasu]] # [[:en:Maeda Toshitsugu|Maeda Toshitsugu]] (1858-1900) # [[:en:Toshinari Maeda|Toshinari Maeda]] (1885-1942) # [[Toshitatsu Maeda]] (1908-1989) # [[Toshiyasu Maeda]] (b.1935) # [[Toshinori Maeda]] (b.1963, Heir) # [[Toshiyuki Maeda]] (2nd generation heir) # [[Toshiyoshi Maeda (President-elect, President and CEO of Inoda Coffee)]]
===Toyama=== # [[:en:Maeda Toshitsugu|Maeda Toshitsugu]] (1617 – 1674) # [[:en:Maeda Masatoshi|Maeda Masatoshi]] (1649 – 1706) # [[:en:Maeda Toshioki|Maeda Toshioki]] (1678 – 1733) # [[:en:Maeda Toshitaka (Toyama)|Maeda Toshitaka]] (1690 – 1745) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiyuki|Maeda Toshiyuki]] (1730 – 1762) # [[:en:Maeda Toshitomo|Maeda Toshitomo]] (1737 – 1794) # [[:en:Maeda Toshihisa|Maeda Toshihisa]] (1762 – 1787) # [[:en:Maeda Toshinori (Toyama)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1768 – 1801) # [[:en:Maeda Toshitsuyo|Maeda Toshitsuyo]] (1772 – 1836) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiyasu (Toyama)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1800-1859) # [[:en:Maeda Toshitomo|Maeda Toshitomo]] (1834 – 1854) # [[:en:Maeda Toshikata|Maeda Toshikata]] (1835 – 1904) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiatsu|Maeda Toshiatsu]] (1856 – 1921) # [[Maeda Toshio (Toyama)|Maeda Toshio]] (1886 – 1966) # [[Maeda Toshinobu]] # [[Maeda Akitoshi]]
===Daishoji=== # [[:en:Maeda Toshiharu|Maeda Toshiharu]] (1618 – 1660) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiaki I|Maeda Toshiaki]] I (1638 – 1692) # [[:en:Maeda Toshinao|Maeda Toshinao]] (1672 – 1711) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiakira|Maeda Toshiakira]] (1691 – 1737) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] I (1733 – 1781) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiaki II|Maeda Toshiaki]] II (1758 – 1791) # [[:en:Maeda Toshitane|Maeda Toshitane]] (1760 – 1788) # [[:en:Maeda Toshiyasu (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshiyasu]] (1779 – 1806) # [[:en:Maeda Toshikore|Maeda Toshikore]] (1785 – 1837) # [[:en:Maeda Toshinaka|Maeda Toshinaka]] (1812 – 1838) # [[:en:Maeda Toshihira|Maeda Toshihira]] (1824 – 1849) # [[:en:Maeda Toshinori (Daishoji)|Maeda Toshinori]] (1833 – 1855) # [[Maeda Toshimichi]] II (1835 – 1855) # [[:en:Maeda Toshika|Maeda Toshika]] (1841 – 1920) # [[Maeda Toshimitsu]] (1905-?) # [[Maeda Toshihiro]] (b.1929)

The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through [[:en:Sugawara no Kiyotomo|Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] and [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]] under the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' (land value).

[[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of [[:en:Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya]]. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of [[:en:Oda Nobuhide|Oda Nobuhide]], who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at [[:en:Kiyosu Castle|Kiyosu Castle]]. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the [[:en:Oda clan|Oda clan]], and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, [[:en:Maeda Toshiie|Maeda Toshiie]].
Another notable member of the family was [[:en:Maeda Toshimasu|Maeda Toshimasu]], commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of [[:en:Takigawa Kazumasu|Takigawa Kazumasu]], he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. He began his career as a [[:en:Page (servant)|page]], rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]]. After defeating the [[:en:Asakura clan|Asakura clan]], he fought under [[:en:Shibata Katsuie|Shibata Katsuie]] in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the [[:en:Ikkō-ikki|Ikkō-ikki]], and participated in the 1570 [[:en:Battle of Anegawa|Battle of Anegawa]] and the 1577 [[:en:Battle of Tedorigawa|Battle of Tedorigawa]]. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in [[:en:Etchū Province|Etchū Province]] (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in [[:en:Kaga Province|Kaga Province]] to form [[:en:Kaga Domain|Kaga Domain]].<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' of well over a million ''koku''. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by [[:en:Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|Maeda Toshitaka]], the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at [[:en:Nanokaichi Domain|Nanokaichi Domain]] in [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Kōzuke Province]]. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''[[:en:O-Ie Sōdō|O-Ie Sōdō]]'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q578033]] Wikidata name: Maeda clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}} |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} clan who occupied most of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region}} of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} and {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}} under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}}, and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of {{ill|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q1155226|lt=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}}. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of {{ill|Oda Nobuhide|en|Oda Nobuhide|WD=Q1153962|lt=Oda Nobuhide}}, who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle}}. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|WD=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan}}, and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, {{ill|Maeda Toshiie|en|Maeda Toshiie|WD=Q1196817|lt=Maeda Toshiie}}.
Another notable member of the family was {{ill|Maeda Toshimasu|en|Maeda Toshimasu|WD=Q1150951|lt=Maeda Toshimasu}}, commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of {{ill|Takigawa Kazumasu|en|Takigawa Kazumasu|WD=Q953624|lt=Takigawa Kazumasu}}, he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|WD=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan}} leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. He began his career as a {{ill|Page (servant)|en|Page (servant)|WD=Q855855|lt=page}}, rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. After defeating the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|WD=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan}}, he fought under {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|Shibata Katsuie|WD=Q707587|lt=Shibata Katsuie}} in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|WD=Q947533|lt=Ikkō-ikki}}, and participated in the 1570 {{ill|Battle of Anegawa|en|Battle of Anegawa|WD=Q964983|lt=Battle of Anegawa}} and the 1577 {{ill|Battle of Tedorigawa|en|Battle of Tedorigawa|WD=Q1045768|lt=Battle of Tedorigawa}}. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|WD=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province}} (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province}} (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|WD=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province}} to form {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}}.<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} and built {{ill|Kanazawa Castle|en|Kanazawa Castle|WD=Q511412|lt=Kanazawa Castle}}; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|WD=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa}} from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|WD=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune}} established two cadet branches of the clan at {{ill|Toyama Domain|en|Toyama Domain|WD=Q1088437|lt=Toyama}} and {{ill|Daishōji Domain|en|Daishōji Domain|WD=Q3034431|lt=Daishōji}}. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by {{ill|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|en|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|WD=Q6729117|lt=Maeda Toshitaka}}, the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at {{ill|Nanokaichi Domain|en|Nanokaichi Domain|WD=Q3034506|lt=Nanokaichi Domain}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''{{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō}}'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q578033]] Wikidata name: Maeda clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}} |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} clan who occupied most of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region}} of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} and {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}} under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}}, and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of {{ill|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q1155226|lt=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}}. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of {{ill|Oda Nobuhide|en|Oda Nobuhide|WD=Q1153962|lt=Oda Nobuhide}}, who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle}}. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|WD=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan}}, and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, {{ill|Maeda Toshiie|en|Maeda Toshiie|WD=Q1196817|lt=Maeda Toshiie}}.
Another notable member of the family was {{ill|Maeda Toshimasu|en|Maeda Toshimasu|WD=Q1150951|lt=Maeda Toshimasu}}, commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of {{ill|Takigawa Kazumasu|en|Takigawa Kazumasu|WD=Q953624|lt=Takigawa Kazumasu}}, he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|WD=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan}} leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. He began his career as a {{ill|Page (servant)|en|Page (servant)|WD=Q855855|lt=page}}, rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. After defeating the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|WD=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan}}, he fought under {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|Shibata Katsuie|WD=Q707587|lt=Shibata Katsuie}} in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|WD=Q947533|lt=Ikkō-ikki}}, and participated in the 1570 {{ill|Battle of Anegawa|en|Battle of Anegawa|WD=Q964983|lt=Battle of Anegawa}} and the 1577 {{ill|Battle of Tedorigawa|en|Battle of Tedorigawa|WD=Q1045768|lt=Battle of Tedorigawa}}. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|WD=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province}} (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province}} (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|WD=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province}} to form {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}}.<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} and built {{ill|Kanazawa Castle|en|Kanazawa Castle|WD=Q511412|lt=Kanazawa Castle}}; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|WD=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa}} from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|WD=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune}} established two cadet branches of the clan at {{ill|Toyama Domain|en|Toyama Domain|WD=Q1088437|lt=Toyama}} and {{ill|Daishōji Domain|en|Daishōji Domain|WD=Q3034431|lt=Daishōji}}. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by {{ill|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|en|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|WD=Q6729117|lt=Maeda Toshitaka}}, the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at {{ill|Nanokaichi Domain|en|Nanokaichi Domain|WD=Q3034506|lt=Nanokaichi Domain}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''{{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō}}'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

Japanese {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} clan who occupied most of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region}} of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} and {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}} under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}}, and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of {{ill|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q1155226|lt=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}}. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of {{ill|Oda Nobuhide|en|Oda Nobuhide|WD=Q1153962|lt=Oda Nobuhide}}, who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle}}. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|WD=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan}}, and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, {{ill|Maeda Toshiie|en|Maeda Toshiie|WD=Q1196817|lt=Maeda Toshiie}}.
Another notable member of the family was {{ill|Maeda Toshimasu|en|Maeda Toshimasu|WD=Q1150951|lt=Maeda Toshimasu}}, commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of {{ill|Takigawa Kazumasu|en|Takigawa Kazumasu|WD=Q953624|lt=Takigawa Kazumasu}}, he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|WD=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan}} leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. He began his career as a {{ill|Page (servant)|en|Page (servant)|WD=Q855855|lt=page}}, rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. After defeating the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|WD=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan}}, he fought under {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|Shibata Katsuie|WD=Q707587|lt=Shibata Katsuie}} in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|WD=Q947533|lt=Ikkō-ikki}}, and participated in the 1570 {{ill|Battle of Anegawa|en|Battle of Anegawa|WD=Q964983|lt=Battle of Anegawa}} and the 1577 {{ill|Battle of Tedorigawa|en|Battle of Tedorigawa|WD=Q1045768|lt=Battle of Tedorigawa}}. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|WD=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province}} (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province}} (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|WD=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province}} to form {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}}.<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} and built {{ill|Kanazawa Castle|en|Kanazawa Castle|WD=Q511412|lt=Kanazawa Castle}}; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|WD=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa}} from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|WD=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune}} established two cadet branches of the clan at {{ill|Toyama Domain|en|Toyama Domain|WD=Q1088437|lt=Toyama}} and {{ill|Daishōji Domain|en|Daishōji Domain|WD=Q3034431|lt=Daishōji}}. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by {{ill|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|en|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|WD=Q6729117|lt=Maeda Toshitaka}}, the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at {{ill|Nanokaichi Domain|en|Nanokaichi Domain|WD=Q3034506|lt=Nanokaichi Domain}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''{{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō}}'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

until = 1873 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} clan who occupied most of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region}} of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} and {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}} under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' (land value).

This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of {{ill|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q1155226|lt=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}}. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} and built {{ill|Kanazawa Castle|en|Kanazawa Castle|WD=Q511412|lt=Kanazawa Castle}}; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|WD=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa}} from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|WD=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune}} established two cadet branches of the clan at {{ill|Toyama Domain|en|Toyama Domain|WD=Q1088437|lt=Toyama}} and {{ill|Daishōji Domain|en|Daishōji Domain|WD=Q3034431|lt=Daishōji}}. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by {{ill|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|en|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|WD=Q6729117|lt=Maeda Toshitaka}}, the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at {{ill|Nanokaichi Domain|en|Nanokaichi Domain|WD=Q3034506|lt=Nanokaichi Domain}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''{{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō}}'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]]
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}} |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} clan who occupied most of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region}} of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} and {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}} under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}}, and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of {{ill|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q1155226|lt=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}}. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of {{ill|Oda Nobuhide|en|Oda Nobuhide|WD=Q1153962|lt=Oda Nobuhide}}, who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle}}. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|WD=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan}}, and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, {{ill|Maeda Toshiie|en|Maeda Toshiie|WD=Q1196817|lt=Maeda Toshiie}}.
Another notable member of the family was {{ill|Maeda Toshimasu|en|Maeda Toshimasu|WD=Q1150951|lt=Maeda Toshimasu}}, commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of {{ill|Takigawa Kazumasu|en|Takigawa Kazumasu|WD=Q953624|lt=Takigawa Kazumasu}}, he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|WD=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan}} leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. He began his career as a {{ill|Page (servant)|en|Page (servant)|WD=Q855855|lt=page}}, rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. After defeating the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|WD=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan}}, he fought under {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|Shibata Katsuie|WD=Q707587|lt=Shibata Katsuie}} in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|WD=Q947533|lt=Ikkō-ikki}}, and participated in the 1570 {{ill|Battle of Anegawa|en|Battle of Anegawa|WD=Q964983|lt=Battle of Anegawa}} and the 1577 {{ill|Battle of Tedorigawa|en|Battle of Tedorigawa|WD=Q1045768|lt=Battle of Tedorigawa}}. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|WD=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province}} (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province}} (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|WD=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province}} to form {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}}.<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} and built {{ill|Kanazawa Castle|en|Kanazawa Castle|WD=Q511412|lt=Kanazawa Castle}}; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|WD=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa}} from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|WD=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune}} established two cadet branches of the clan at {{ill|Toyama Domain|en|Toyama Domain|WD=Q1088437|lt=Toyama}} and {{ill|Daishōji Domain|en|Daishōji Domain|WD=Q3034431|lt=Daishōji}}. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by {{ill|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|en|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|WD=Q6729117|lt=Maeda Toshitaka}}, the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at {{ill|Nanokaichi Domain|en|Nanokaichi Domain|WD=Q3034506|lt=Nanokaichi Domain}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''{{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō}}'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.
