revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]]
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}} |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} clan who occupied most of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region}} of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} and {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}} under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}}, and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of {{ill|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q1155226|lt=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}}. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of {{ill|Oda Nobuhide|en|Oda Nobuhide|WD=Q1153962|lt=Oda Nobuhide}}, who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle}}. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|WD=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan}}, and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, {{ill|Maeda Toshiie|en|Maeda Toshiie|WD=Q1196817|lt=Maeda Toshiie}}.
Another notable member of the family was {{ill|Maeda Toshimasu|en|Maeda Toshimasu|WD=Q1150951|lt=Maeda Toshimasu}}, commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of {{ill|Takigawa Kazumasu|en|Takigawa Kazumasu|WD=Q953624|lt=Takigawa Kazumasu}}, he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|WD=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan}} leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. He began his career as a {{ill|Page (servant)|en|Page (servant)|WD=Q855855|lt=page}}, rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. After defeating the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|WD=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan}}, he fought under {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|Shibata Katsuie|WD=Q707587|lt=Shibata Katsuie}} in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|WD=Q947533|lt=Ikkō-ikki}}, and participated in the 1570 {{ill|Battle of Anegawa|en|Battle of Anegawa|WD=Q964983|lt=Battle of Anegawa}} and the 1577 {{ill|Battle of Tedorigawa|en|Battle of Tedorigawa|WD=Q1045768|lt=Battle of Tedorigawa}}. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|WD=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province}} (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province}} (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|WD=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province}} to form {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}}.<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} and built {{ill|Kanazawa Castle|en|Kanazawa Castle|WD=Q511412|lt=Kanazawa Castle}}; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|WD=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa}} from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|WD=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune}} established two cadet branches of the clan at {{ill|Toyama Domain|en|Toyama Domain|WD=Q1088437|lt=Toyama}} and {{ill|Daishōji Domain|en|Daishōji Domain|WD=Q3034431|lt=Daishōji}}. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by {{ill|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|en|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|WD=Q6729117|lt=Maeda Toshitaka}}, the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at {{ill|Nanokaichi Domain|en|Nanokaichi Domain|WD=Q3034506|lt=Nanokaichi Domain}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''{{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō}}'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]]
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}} |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}} |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} clan who occupied most of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region}} of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} and {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}} under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province}}, and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of {{ill|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q1155226|lt=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}}. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of {{ill|Oda Nobuhide|en|Oda Nobuhide|WD=Q1153962|lt=Oda Nobuhide}}, who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle}}. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|WD=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan}}, and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, {{ill|Maeda Toshiie|en|Maeda Toshiie|WD=Q1196817|lt=Maeda Toshiie}}.
Another notable member of the family was {{ill|Maeda Toshimasu|en|Maeda Toshimasu|WD=Q1150951|lt=Maeda Toshimasu}}, commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of {{ill|Takigawa Kazumasu|en|Takigawa Kazumasu|WD=Q953624|lt=Takigawa Kazumasu}}, he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|WD=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan}} leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga}}. He began his career as a {{ill|Page (servant)|en|Page (servant)|WD=Q855855|lt=page}}, rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. After defeating the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|WD=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan}}, he fought under {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|Shibata Katsuie|WD=Q707587|lt=Shibata Katsuie}} in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|WD=Q947533|lt=Ikkō-ikki}}, and participated in the 1570 {{ill|Battle of Anegawa|en|Battle of Anegawa|WD=Q964983|lt=Battle of Anegawa}} and the 1577 {{ill|Battle of Tedorigawa|en|Battle of Tedorigawa|WD=Q1045768|lt=Battle of Tedorigawa}}. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|WD=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province}} (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province}} (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|WD=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province}} to form {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain}}.<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} and built {{ill|Kanazawa Castle|en|Kanazawa Castle|WD=Q511412|lt=Kanazawa Castle}}; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|WD=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa}} from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|WD=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune}} established two cadet branches of the clan at {{ill|Toyama Domain|en|Toyama Domain|WD=Q1088437|lt=Toyama}} and {{ill|Daishōji Domain|en|Daishōji Domain|WD=Q3034431|lt=Daishōji}}. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by {{ill|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|en|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|WD=Q6729117|lt=Maeda Toshitaka}}, the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at {{ill|Nanokaichi Domain|en|Nanokaichi Domain|WD=Q3034506|lt=Nanokaichi Domain}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province}}. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''{{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō}}'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}} |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system|1=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} clan who occupied most of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region|1=Hokuriku region}} of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo|1=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} and {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain|1=Kaga Domain}} under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of {{ill|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q1155226|lt=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|1=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}}. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of {{ill|Oda Nobuhide|en|Oda Nobuhide|WD=Q1153962|lt=Oda Nobuhide|1=Oda Nobuhide}}, who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle|1=Kiyosu Castle}}. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|WD=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan|1=Oda clan}}, and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, {{ill|Maeda Toshiie|en|Maeda Toshiie|WD=Q1196817|lt=Maeda Toshiie|1=Maeda Toshiie}}.
Another notable member of the family was {{ill|Maeda Toshimasu|en|Maeda Toshimasu|WD=Q1150951|lt=Maeda Toshimasu|1=Maeda Toshimasu}}, commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of {{ill|Takigawa Kazumasu|en|Takigawa Kazumasu|WD=Q953624|lt=Takigawa Kazumasu|1=Takigawa Kazumasu}}, he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|WD=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan|1=Mogami clan}} leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. He began his career as a {{ill|Page (servant)|en|Page (servant)|WD=Q855855|lt=page|1=Page (servant)}}, rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. After defeating the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|WD=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan|1=Asakura clan}}, he fought under {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|Shibata Katsuie|WD=Q707587|lt=Shibata Katsuie|1=Shibata Katsuie}} in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|WD=Q947533|lt=Ikkō-ikki|1=Ikkō-ikki}}, and participated in the 1570 {{ill|Battle of Anegawa|en|Battle of Anegawa|WD=Q964983|lt=Battle of Anegawa|1=Battle of Anegawa}} and the 1577 {{ill|Battle of Tedorigawa|en|Battle of Tedorigawa|WD=Q1045768|lt=Battle of Tedorigawa|1=Battle of Tedorigawa}}. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|WD=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province|1=Etchū Province}} (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province|1=Noto Province}} (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|WD=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province|1=Kaga Province}} to form {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain|1=Kaga Domain}}.<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}} and built {{ill|Kanazawa Castle|en|Kanazawa Castle|WD=Q511412|lt=Kanazawa Castle|1=Kanazawa Castle}}; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|WD=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa|1=Kanazawa}} from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|WD=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune|1=Maeda Toshitsune}} established two cadet branches of the clan at {{ill|Toyama Domain|en|Toyama Domain|WD=Q1088437|lt=Toyama|1=Toyama Domain}} and {{ill|Daishōji Domain|en|Daishōji Domain|WD=Q3034431|lt=Daishōji|1=Daishōji Domain}}. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by {{ill|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|en|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|WD=Q6729117|lt=Maeda Toshitaka|1=Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)}}, the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at {{ill|Nanokaichi Domain|en|Nanokaichi Domain|WD=Q3034506|lt=Nanokaichi Domain|1=Nanokaichi Domain}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}}. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''{{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō|1=O-Ie Sōdō}}'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}} |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system|1=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} clan who occupied most of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region|1=Hokuriku region}} of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo|1=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} and {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain|1=Kaga Domain}} under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of {{ill|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q1155226|lt=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|1=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}}. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of {{ill|Oda Nobuhide|en|Oda Nobuhide|WD=Q1153962|lt=Oda Nobuhide|1=Oda Nobuhide}}, who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle|1=Kiyosu Castle}}. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|WD=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan|1=Oda clan}}, and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, {{ill|Maeda Toshiie|en|Maeda Toshiie|WD=Q1196817|lt=Maeda Toshiie|1=Maeda Toshiie}}.
Another notable member of the family was {{ill|Maeda Toshimasu|en|Maeda Toshimasu|WD=Q1150951|lt=Maeda Toshimasu|1=Maeda Toshimasu}}, commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of {{ill|Takigawa Kazumasu|en|Takigawa Kazumasu|WD=Q953624|lt=Takigawa Kazumasu|1=Takigawa Kazumasu}}, he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|WD=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan|1=Mogami clan}} leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. He began his career as a {{ill|Page (servant)|en|Page (servant)|WD=Q855855|lt=page|1=Page (servant)}}, rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. After defeating the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|WD=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan|1=Asakura clan}}, he fought under {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|Shibata Katsuie|WD=Q707587|lt=Shibata Katsuie|1=Shibata Katsuie}} in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|WD=Q947533|lt=Ikkō-ikki|1=Ikkō-ikki}}, and participated in the 1570 {{ill|Battle of Anegawa|en|Battle of Anegawa|WD=Q964983|lt=Battle of Anegawa|1=Battle of Anegawa}} and the 1577 {{ill|Battle of Tedorigawa|en|Battle of Tedorigawa|WD=Q1045768|lt=Battle of Tedorigawa|1=Battle of Tedorigawa}}. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|WD=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province|1=Etchū Province}} (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province|1=Noto Province}} (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|WD=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province|1=Kaga Province}} to form {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain|1=Kaga Domain}}.<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}} and built {{ill|Kanazawa Castle|en|Kanazawa Castle|WD=Q511412|lt=Kanazawa Castle|1=Kanazawa Castle}}; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|WD=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa|1=Kanazawa}} from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|WD=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune|1=Maeda Toshitsune}} established two cadet branches of the clan at {{ill|Toyama Domain|en|Toyama Domain|WD=Q1088437|lt=Toyama|1=Toyama Domain}} and {{ill|Daishōji Domain|en|Daishōji Domain|WD=Q3034431|lt=Daishōji|1=Daishōji Domain}}. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by {{ill|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|en|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|WD=Q6729117|lt=Maeda Toshitaka|1=Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)}}, the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at {{ill|Nanokaichi Domain|en|Nanokaichi Domain|WD=Q3034506|lt=Nanokaichi Domain|1=Nanokaichi Domain}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}}. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''{{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō|1=O-Ie Sōdō}}'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]]
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}} |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system|1=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} clan who occupied most of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|WD=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region|1=Hokuriku region}} of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo|1=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} and {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain|1=Kaga Domain}} under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|WD=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|WD=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of {{ill|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|WD=Q1155226|lt=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|1=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}}. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of {{ill|Oda Nobuhide|en|Oda Nobuhide|WD=Q1153962|lt=Oda Nobuhide|1=Oda Nobuhide}}, who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|WD=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle|1=Kiyosu Castle}}. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|WD=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan|1=Oda clan}}, and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, {{ill|Maeda Toshiie|en|Maeda Toshiie|WD=Q1196817|lt=Maeda Toshiie|1=Maeda Toshiie}}.
Another notable member of the family was {{ill|Maeda Toshimasu|en|Maeda Toshimasu|WD=Q1150951|lt=Maeda Toshimasu|1=Maeda Toshimasu}}, commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of {{ill|Takigawa Kazumasu|en|Takigawa Kazumasu|WD=Q953624|lt=Takigawa Kazumasu|1=Takigawa Kazumasu}}, he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|WD=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan|1=Mogami clan}} leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|WD=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. He began his career as a {{ill|Page (servant)|en|Page (servant)|WD=Q855855|lt=page|1=Page (servant)}}, rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. After defeating the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|WD=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan|1=Asakura clan}}, he fought under {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|Shibata Katsuie|WD=Q707587|lt=Shibata Katsuie|1=Shibata Katsuie}} in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|WD=Q947533|lt=Ikkō-ikki|1=Ikkō-ikki}}, and participated in the 1570 {{ill|Battle of Anegawa|en|Battle of Anegawa|WD=Q964983|lt=Battle of Anegawa|1=Battle of Anegawa}} and the 1577 {{ill|Battle of Tedorigawa|en|Battle of Tedorigawa|WD=Q1045768|lt=Battle of Tedorigawa|1=Battle of Tedorigawa}}. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|WD=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province|1=Etchū Province}} (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province|1=Noto Province}} (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|WD=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province|1=Kaga Province}} to form {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|WD=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain|1=Kaga Domain}}.<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}} and built {{ill|Kanazawa Castle|en|Kanazawa Castle|WD=Q511412|lt=Kanazawa Castle|1=Kanazawa Castle}}; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|WD=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa|1=Kanazawa}} from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|WD=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune|1=Maeda Toshitsune}} established two cadet branches of the clan at {{ill|Toyama Domain|en|Toyama Domain|WD=Q1088437|lt=Toyama|1=Toyama Domain}} and {{ill|Daishōji Domain|en|Daishōji Domain|WD=Q3034431|lt=Daishōji|1=Daishōji Domain}}. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by {{ill|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|en|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|WD=Q6729117|lt=Maeda Toshitaka|1=Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)}}, the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at {{ill|Nanokaichi Domain|en|Nanokaichi Domain|WD=Q3034506|lt=Nanokaichi Domain|1=Nanokaichi Domain}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}}. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''{{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|WD=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō|1=O-Ie Sōdō}}'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:عشيرة ماييدا]][[ckb:خێڵی مایێدا]][[de:Maeda (Klan)]][[en:Maeda clan]][[eo:Maeda (klano)]][[fa:خاندان مائدا]][[fr:Clan Maeda]][[it:Clan Maeda]][[ja:前田氏]][[ko:마에다씨]][[pt:Clã Maeda]][[ru:Маэда (род)]][[uk:Рід Маеда]][[zh:前田氏]]
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|qid=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}} |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|qid=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system|1=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} clan who occupied most of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|qid=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region|1=Hokuriku region}} of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|qid=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo|1=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} and {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|qid=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain|1=Kaga Domain}} under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|qid=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|qid=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|qid=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of {{ill|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|qid=Q1155226|lt=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|1=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}}. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of {{ill|Oda Nobuhide|en|Oda Nobuhide|qid=Q1153962|lt=Oda Nobuhide|1=Oda Nobuhide}}, who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|qid=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle|1=Kiyosu Castle}}. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|qid=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan|1=Oda clan}}, and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, {{ill|Maeda Toshiie|en|Maeda Toshiie|qid=Q1196817|lt=Maeda Toshiie|1=Maeda Toshiie}}.
Another notable member of the family was {{ill|Maeda Toshimasu|en|Maeda Toshimasu|qid=Q1150951|lt=Maeda Toshimasu|1=Maeda Toshimasu}}, commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of {{ill|Takigawa Kazumasu|en|Takigawa Kazumasu|qid=Q953624|lt=Takigawa Kazumasu|1=Takigawa Kazumasu}}, he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|qid=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan|1=Mogami clan}} leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. He began his career as a {{ill|Page (servant)|en|Page (servant)|qid=Q855855|lt=page|1=Page (servant)}}, rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. After defeating the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|qid=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan|1=Asakura clan}}, he fought under {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|Shibata Katsuie|qid=Q707587|lt=Shibata Katsuie|1=Shibata Katsuie}} in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|qid=Q947533|lt=Ikkō-ikki|1=Ikkō-ikki}}, and participated in the 1570 {{ill|Battle of Anegawa|en|Battle of Anegawa|qid=Q964983|lt=Battle of Anegawa|1=Battle of Anegawa}} and the 1577 {{ill|Battle of Tedorigawa|en|Battle of Tedorigawa|qid=Q1045768|lt=Battle of Tedorigawa|1=Battle of Tedorigawa}}. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|qid=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province|1=Etchū Province}} (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|qid=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province|1=Noto Province}} (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|qid=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province|1=Kaga Province}} to form {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|qid=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain|1=Kaga Domain}}.<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|qid=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|qid=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}} and built {{ill|Kanazawa Castle|en|Kanazawa Castle|qid=Q511412|lt=Kanazawa Castle|1=Kanazawa Castle}}; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|qid=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa|1=Kanazawa}} from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|qid=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune|1=Maeda Toshitsune}} established two cadet branches of the clan at {{ill|Toyama Domain|en|Toyama Domain|qid=Q1088437|lt=Toyama|1=Toyama Domain}} and {{ill|Daishōji Domain|en|Daishōji Domain|qid=Q3034431|lt=Daishōji|1=Daishōji Domain}}. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by {{ill|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|en|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|qid=Q6729117|lt=Maeda Toshitaka|1=Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)}}, the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at {{ill|Nanokaichi Domain|en|Nanokaichi Domain|qid=Q3034506|lt=Nanokaichi Domain|1=Nanokaichi Domain}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|qid=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}}. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''{{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|qid=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō|1=O-Ie Sōdō}}'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.

revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese samurai clan}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Maeda clan |surname nihongo = 前田氏 |image = Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg |caption = Maeda [[Mon (emblem)|clan emblem]] |province of origin = {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|qid=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}} |parent house = [[File:Japanese crest Kaga Umebachi.svg|15px]] [[Sugawara clan]] |titles = Various |founder = unknown |final ruler = |current head = |founding year = {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} |dissolution = still extant |ruled until = 1873 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|qid=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system|1=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches = }} The {{nihongo|'''Maeda clan'''|前田氏|Maeda-shi}} was a Japanese {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} clan who occupied most of the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|qid=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region|1=Hokuriku region}} of central [[Honshu|Honshū]] from the end of the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} through the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] of 1868. The Maeda claimed descent from the [[Sugawara clan]] through {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|qid=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo|1=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} and {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} in the eighth and ninth centuries; however, the line of descent is uncertain. The Maeda rose to prominence as ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' of {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|qid=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain|1=Kaga Domain}} under the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} [[Tokugawa shogunate]], which was second only to the [[Tokugawa clan]] in ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|qid=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' (land value).
==Origins== "Maeda" is a place name in Kaitō District of western {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|qid=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, and was the seat of the senior branch of the Maeda clan in the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|qid=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}. [[Maeda Nagatane]] (1550-1631) entered into the service of Maeda Toshiie, and his descendants became hereditary retainers of the Maeda clan of Kaga Domain. This branch received the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage title of ''danshaku'' (baron) after the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]].
A cadet branch of the Owari Maeda were given the castle of Arako in what is now part of {{ill|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|en|Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|qid=Q1155226|lt=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya|1=Nakagawa-ku, Nagoya}}. [[Maeda Toshimasa]] (d.1560) entered the service of {{ill|Oda Nobuhide|en|Oda Nobuhide|qid=Q1153962|lt=Oda Nobuhide|1=Oda Nobuhide}}, who nominally ruled Owari Province from his seat at {{ill|Kiyosu Castle|en|Kiyosu Castle|qid=Q1357421|lt=Kiyosu Castle|1=Kiyosu Castle}}. His son, Maeda Toshihisa (d.1587) also served the {{ill|Oda clan|en|Oda clan|qid=Q1132077|lt=Oda clan|1=Oda clan}}, and was ordered to retire in favour of his brother, {{ill|Maeda Toshiie|en|Maeda Toshiie|qid=Q1196817|lt=Maeda Toshiie|1=Maeda Toshiie}}.
Another notable member of the family was {{ill|Maeda Toshimasu|en|Maeda Toshimasu|qid=Q1150951|lt=Maeda Toshimasu|1=Maeda Toshimasu}}, commonly known as Maeda Keiji. Though he was biologically the son of {{ill|Takigawa Kazumasu|en|Takigawa Kazumasu|qid=Q953624|lt=Takigawa Kazumasu|1=Takigawa Kazumasu}}, he was adopted by Maeda Toshihisa, the older brother of Maeda Toshiie. He was recognized as a renowned warrior. According to legend, he broke the front line of the {{ill|Mogami clan|en|Mogami clan|qid=Q524625|lt=Mogami clan|1=Mogami clan}} leading a group of just eight riders during a battle in which he fought for the [[Uesugi clan]].
==Sengoku and Edo period== Maeda Toshiie was one of the leading generals under {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}. He began his career as a {{ill|Page (servant)|en|Page (servant)|qid=Q855855|lt=page|1=Page (servant)}}, rising through the Ranks a member of the ''akahoro-shū'' (赤母衣衆), under Nobunaga's personal command and later became an infantry captain (''ashigaru taishō'' 足軽大将). From his youth, he was a close confidant of Nobunaga and a friend of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}. After defeating the {{ill|Asakura clan|en|Asakura clan|qid=Q1772267|lt=Asakura clan|1=Asakura clan}}, he fought under {{ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|Shibata Katsuie|qid=Q707587|lt=Shibata Katsuie|1=Shibata Katsuie}} in the Hokuriku region in the suppression of the {{ill|Ikkō-ikki|en|Ikkō-ikki|qid=Q947533|lt=Ikkō-ikki|1=Ikkō-ikki}}, and participated in the 1570 {{ill|Battle of Anegawa|en|Battle of Anegawa|qid=Q964983|lt=Battle of Anegawa|1=Battle of Anegawa}} and the 1577 {{ill|Battle of Tedorigawa|en|Battle of Tedorigawa|qid=Q1045768|lt=Battle of Tedorigawa|1=Battle of Tedorigawa}}. He was eventually granted the fief of Fuchu in {{ill|Etchū Province|en|Etchū Province|qid=Q906899|lt=Etchū Province|1=Etchū Province}} (30,000 ''koku''), and in 1581 was given {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|qid=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province|1=Noto Province}} (230,000 ''koku''), to which he a11ed his other territories in {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|qid=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province|1=Kaga Province}} to form {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|qid=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain|1=Kaga Domain}}.<ref name="Cassell">{{cite book|last1=Turnbull|first1=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook|date=2000|publisher=Cassell & C0|location=London|isbn=1854095234|page=55,228}}</ref> After Nobunaga's death, he pledged fealty to Toyotomi Hideyoshi, and his territories were expanded to cover all of the three provinces of Noto, Kaga and Etchū, with a ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|qid=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka|1=Kokudaka}}'' of well over a million ''koku''. Toshiie divided his fief among his sons. His eldest son [[Maeda Toshinaga]] participated in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|qid=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}} and built {{ill|Kanazawa Castle|en|Kanazawa Castle|qid=Q511412|lt=Kanazawa Castle|1=Kanazawa Castle}}; he also was recognised as ''daimyō'' of Kaga Domain under the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].
The Maeda clan attempted to maintain good relations with the Tokugawa clan through marriage ties, and, although a [[:en:tozama daimyo|''tozama'']] clan, were permitted to use the "Matsudaira" name as an honorific patronym. The Maeda clan continued to rule Kaga Domain from their headquarters in {{ill|Kanazawa|en|Kanazawa|qid=Q191130|lt=Kanazawa|1=Kanazawa}} from 1583 until the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]] in 1868. {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|qid=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune|1=Maeda Toshitsune}} established two cadet branches of the clan at {{ill|Toyama Domain|en|Toyama Domain|qid=Q1088437|lt=Toyama|1=Toyama Domain}} and {{ill|Daishōji Domain|en|Daishōji Domain|qid=Q3034431|lt=Daishōji|1=Daishōji Domain}}. Another cadet branch of the clan was established by {{ill|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|en|Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)|qid=Q6729117|lt=Maeda Toshitaka|1=Maeda Toshitaka (Nanokaichi)}}, the fifth son of Maeda Toshiie, at {{ill|Nanokaichi Domain|en|Nanokaichi Domain|qid=Q3034506|lt=Nanokaichi Domain|1=Nanokaichi Domain}} in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|qid=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}}. All of these cadet branches also continued to be ruled by the Maeda clan until the Meiji restoration. However, the Maeda clan was often beset by ''{{ill|O-Ie Sōdō|en|O-Ie Sōdō|qid=Q133766|lt=O-Ie Sōdō|1=O-Ie Sōdō}}'' incidents, and many of the clan heads died young, or without heir. The clan did not play a prominent role in the [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji restoration]]. After the start of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the former heads of the various branches of the Maeda clan were made peers under the ''[[kazoku]]'' peerage system.
